Title: Up-regulation of lipid biosynthesis increases the oil content in leaves of Sorghum bicolor

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low or absent transcript levels in most transgenic sorghum leaf lines.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy-dense storage oil for food, feed, oleochemical and biofuel applications.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf cytosolic lipid droplets, mainly mesophyll cells in high-oil lines.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Included to stabilize oil bodies, but in this study its low expression did not correlate with TAG accumulation and likely contributed little to the observed oil increase.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression in sorghum; ddPCR expression analysis; correlation with TAG/TFA; microscopy of lipid droplets.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suboptimal expression may limit oil droplet coating and promote futile TAG turnover, especially in senescing leaves.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Improving expression of Oleosin-L or alternative oil-body proteins could further stabilize droplets and raise leaf TAG in future engineering.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf developmental stage affected persistence of accumulated oil; poor oil-body coating may matter more during senescence.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Sesamum indicum Oleosin-L was co-introduced as an oil-body structural factor. 2. Its transcript level was low in most lines and did not correlate with TAG or TFA content. 3. The study infers that inadequate oleosin expression may have limited droplet stability and final oil accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as a non-decisive component under current expression conditions; authors propose alternative oil-body proteins such as caleosin or LDAPs for future improvement.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Kennedy pathway; terminal acylation step of TAG assembly
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol at the sn-3 position using acyl-CoA to form triacylglycerol.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expressed in leaves of transgenic sorghum; expression positively correlated with TAG and TFA at boot leaf stage.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy-dense storage oil for food, feed, oleochemical and biofuel applications.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf mesophyll cell cytosolic lipid droplets; highest in flag leaf at seed setting stage.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes strong TAG accumulation in sorghum leaves when co-expressed with WRI1; DGAT2a expression strongly positively correlates with TAG content.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in sorghum; ddPCR expression analysis; TAG/TFA quantification by TLC-GC-FID; LC-MS lipidomics; correlation analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key driver of TAG biosynthesis in combination with WRI1; terminal control point for TAG assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Associated engineered lines showed smaller tillers in several events, altered leaf lipidome and fatty acid composition; some lines showed sterility/high transgene copy number, though not attributed solely to DGAT2a.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering high-oil vegetative biomass in sorghum; should be stacked with WRI1 and possibly improved oil-body protection or reduced TAG turnover for higher accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Zea mays Ubiquitin-1 promoter driving UrDGAT2a (constitutive)
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glasshouse growth; leaf developmental stage strongly affected TAG level, with highest values at seed setting/flag leaf.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UrDGAT2a overexpression together with WRI1 enabled major TAG accumulation in sorghum leaves. 2. DGAT2a transcript abundance showed strong positive correlation with TAG content, especially in PEPC:WRI1 lines. 3. DGAT2a likely catalyzed the terminal DAG-to-TAG step underlying altered TAG molecular species profiles.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Separate expression of pOIL197 alone caused only small TAG/TFA increases; paper discusses possible substrate preference contributing to palmitate-rich TAG in some lines, but this was not directly biochemically tested.

Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expressed in leaves; either constitutive across leaf tissues under Ubiquitin-1 promoter or mesophyll-biased/light-associated under PEPC promoter.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy-dense storage oil for food, feed, oleochemical and biofuel applications.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf mesophyll cell cytosolic lipid droplets; especially prominent in PEPC:WRI1 line 03-31.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Fatty acid and TAG biosynthesis; carbon reallocation from carbohydrates toward oil accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Fatty acid and lipid biosynthetic genes; discussed candidate includes FatA; direct sorghum targets not individually validated in this paper.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates lipid biosynthetic program that increases precursor supply for TAG synthesis; likely up-regulates fatty acid synthesis-related genes.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Promoter choice imposed expression domain: ZmUbiquitin-1 constitutive or ZmPEPC mesophyll/light-associated.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly promotes TAG accumulation in sorghum leaves when co-expressed with DGAT2a; mesophyll-specific expression redistributes carbon and shifts lipid/carbohydrate metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in sorghum using two promoters; ddPCR expression analysis; TAG/TFA quantification; LC-MS lipidomics; starch and soluble sugar measurements; GC-MS metabolomics; confocal and SEM imaging; correlation analysis.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key driver of engineered leaf oil accumulation; WRI1 transcript abundance significantly positively correlates with TAG content and promoter choice shapes metabolic outcome.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf developmental stage and promoter context influenced phenotype; PEPC promoter restricted expression mainly to mesophyll cells under light conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Zea mays Ubiquitin-1 promoter (constitutive); Zea mays PEPC promoter (mesophyll-specific/light-associated)
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In PEPC:WRI1 lines, transitory starch and soluble sugars were strongly reduced; broader metabolome shifts occurred, including altered amino acids and organic acids; some transgenic lines were smaller and/or partially sterile.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Central regulatory tool for engineering high-oil biomass sorghum; best used in combination with DGAT2a and additional strategies to stabilize lipid droplets or limit TAG turnover.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Maize WRI1 overexpression in sorghum leaves was essential for high TAG accumulation when combined with DGAT2a. 2. WRI1 expression significantly correlated with TAG and TFA levels. 3. Mesophyll-targeted WRI1 expression caused abundant mesophyll lipid droplets and stronger depletion of starch/sugars, indicating carbon reallocation to oil.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Transformation with WRI1 alone produced only small TAG/TFA increases, supporting synergy with DGAT2a rather than strong standalone effect in sorghum leaves.
